author: @mmdnarayanan
text:
01:06 mathematics as a language.... i keep comoing across people talking about different things as ultimately a language. so fascinated and also confused by it sometimes, when it doenst fully click what the person says as a result, despite me knowing that theres useful knowledge and understanding hidden there. 
can you make a list of things you know are language for things? THIS IS AN OPEN REQUEST.

for eg, balance sheets are a language for money. Maths is a language for shape size and order. language is the language for relationship between things ideas and concepts. 

im ESPECIALLY curious about your personal understanding if you feel it exists on a intuitive level.

author: @lasentinal
text:
Another great presentation. 

I teach my students that pronumerals are just question marks. We have different question marks, so we need different symbols, hence co-ordinate geometry uses x, y, & z, as the most common symbols in 3D geometry.

author: @DavidPaulNewtonScott
text: Google ten to the power one hundred if I remember rightly.

author: @DavidPaulNewtonScott
text:
I am a Physics teacher  and I teach mathematics as a second subject. I learn languages by just thinking of them like mathematics. Eg Mainit pagkain (hot food) , coefficient X value (3x)
I find my ability in mathematics improves when I'm learning a language and vice versa.

author: @djorfuusk
text:
4:43 This.
To all the people out there learning foreign language, speaking to people in a foreign language is not the classroom, in that there’s no penalty for imperfect grammar, save a possible weird look or a giggle... Be jovial about it, and you’ll most likely gain more help that you could ask for as well as possibly new friends 

Example if a smoke show or a super upbeat bro came up to you asked you (in terribly broken *language*) where is the nearest coffee shop, would you tell them to piss off or would you smile and tell them (or walk them) to the coffee shop? Smile and be super outgoingly confident speaking like an caveman at first; it’s faster, it’s fun, it’ll build the confidence, super builds your knowledge base, and you’ll most likely meet some pretty solid people along the way and have  experiences you never would have encountered
TLDR: be confident in speaking a foreign language like a caveman at first... doors will open.

author: @EastBayFlipper
text:
I wish I had seen this video in elementary school 😏
I had brief glimpses of understand with math in grade 5 but I had trouble because I couldn't grasp it because I was trying to choke it down in one bite and couldn't break it up. 
I flunked high school math and  physics 
I had a full scholarship I wasn't prepared to use🤦🏻‍♂️
I would have crashed and burned
I went to vocational school for😂 heavy dirty equipment repair and had a wonderful career until I had an accident and had to return to university,  where I did my trade qualification blocks for interprovincial journeyman red seal.
I went into M 14:40 Mechanical Engineering Technology and I encountered math again, and professors who could teach tradesman and understanding happened. 
Dean's list for Physics, Calculus and statistics and a Canada Scholarship in Technology. 

All because I had someone who could teach the language of math😏

author: @jacobscrackers98
text:
This is completely wrong.
Infinity doesn't just mean "many". It is a specific concept. It doesn't just mean " anything past a googolplex'. I don't know how you can think that.
If maths is about "shape, size, and order", how do you describe other quantities? Are the capacitances of those capacitors you used as a prop a shape, size, or order?
Also, aren't you confusing mathematics with mathematical notation?

text: Thanks.

author: @Alkimi
text:
The mystical process involved in mathematics and language in general... is the creation process. 
When you speak words, real change DOES occur. Emotions and thoughts manifest in the minds of all who hear. This is the result of the magickal quality of language that we imbued it with when we created it, and it has a cascading chain reaction effect. Even if the person doesn't understand the language spoken, it is recognized as language.  Even a dog will recognize the special significance of that which is "spoken". Of course, a dog mostly only recognizes the emotional tone therein, and that is precisely from whence the meaning of words and sounds was derived, long, long ago.

author: @Alkimi
text: Hear here!

author: @peterclark6290
text: The danger of course is that Math is still a human language, and as a species we do tend to exaggerate our level of mastery over all natural forces. E.g. the use of the zero: for in the Cosmos there is no occurrence of a zero anywhere, all is a continuum of forces large and small in permanent 'competition'. But we happily gloss over reality if it reinforces our more majestic impulses. That is not to say Math is not very very useful but it is flawed, is reliant on an ever-expanding understanding of our potential within the much greater potential of a functioning limitless Cosmos with minor outbreaks of lone Universes here and there. Leading to Set Theory, claims of Multi-phenomena, Black this-that-and-the-other, etc. Avoid madness, treat Math proficiency as an early warning.

author: @charleselertii6187
text:
I have a Bachelors of Science Mechanical Engineering.  In University, there was this saying going around:
In Algebra, you learn your Arithmetic.
In Trigonometry, you learn your Algebra.
In Calculus, you learn your Trigonometry.
In Differential Equations, you learn your Calculus.
Nobody ever really learns Differential Equations.......

Cheers, Chuck in Florida.
